claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 2 children:
  - Alex is 4 years old and does not live with you.
  - Blake is 14 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 4 (under 16)
  - Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

Regarding Blake:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 14 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 4 (under 16); Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Claimant contributes £50.00/week towards child's upkeep (>= £26.05) and no other claimaint has priority
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Blake
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 14 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment for you.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Let's begin the Child Benefit eligibility assessment.

**First, I need to know about the child (or children) you're claiming for:**

How old is the child you want to claim Child Benefit for?

- **Under 16 years old**
- **16 to 19 years old**
- **20 years old or over**

If you have multiple children, we can go through each one - just let me know their ages!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I'm looking to claim for my two kids. Alex is 4 years old, and Blake is 14.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hi! You're welcome, I'm happy to help you check your eligibility for Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake.

Great news on the first part of the assessment - since both children are under 16 years old (Alex is 4 and Blake is 14), they automatically meet the age requirement for Child Benefit.

Let me continue with the next check for children under 16.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Are both Alex and Blake working less than 24 hours per week in paid employment (or not working at all)?

Given their ages (4 and 14), I'd expect the answer is yes - but I just need to confirm this, particularly for Blake who is 14.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them work at all. Alex is only 4, and Blake doesn't have a job.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect, that makes sense! Since neither Alex nor Blake are working, they meet this requirement.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Are both Alex and Blake **not** receiving certain benefits in their own right, such as:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit

In other words, neither child is claiming these benefits themselves?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them get any benefits in their own name.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, that's another requirement met. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question:**

Is either Alex or Blake currently doing an apprenticeship?

Given their ages (4 and 14), I'd expect neither is in an apprenticeship - but just to confirm:
- Alex (4) - not in an apprenticeship?
- Blake (14) - not in an apprenticeship?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, neither of them are doing apprenticeships. They're too young for that.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Exactly right! Let me continue to the next check, which will now be about you as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Now let's check your residency status.**

To be eligible for Child Benefit, you need to meet one of the following residency requirements. Do **any** of these apply to you?

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. **You have settled status** under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. **You have pre-settled status** and are:
   - Working (and earning above the National Insurance threshold for 3+ continuous months), OR
   - Actively seeking work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days), OR
   - A student with sufficient resources to support yourself, OR
   - Have sufficient resources to support your family (and not claiming certain means-tested benefits), OR
   - A family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
4. **You're a Crown servant** living abroad (e.g., working for the UK government overseas)

Which of these describes your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: I live in the UK, yes. I'm here in England.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great, you meet the residency requirement since you normally live in the UK. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about your responsibility for the children:**

Do you meet **any** of the following for both Alex and Blake?

1. **You live with the child** - Alex and Blake live with you in your home
2. **You contribute to their upkeep** - You pay at least the equivalent of Child Benefit towards their care (e.g., food, clothes, pocket money, housing)
3. **You pay maintenance** - You make maintenance payments under a court order or agreement

Which of these applies to your situation with Alex and Blake?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home. Alex doesn't live with me, but I help with clothes and things like that for Alex.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. So:

- **Blake (14)** - lives with you ✓
- **Alex (4)** - you contribute to their upkeep (clothes, etc.) ✓

Both of these count as being "responsible" for the children under Child Benefit rules. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Important question about other claimants:**

Is anyone else currently claiming (or planning to claim) Child Benefit for Alex or Blake?

For example:
- For **Alex** (who doesn't live with you) - is the person Alex lives with claiming Child Benefit for them?
- For **Blake** - is anyone else claiming Child Benefit for Blake?

This is important because only one person can receive Child Benefit for each child at a time.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming for Blake since he lives with me. And for Alex, nobody else is getting Child Benefit for him either.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Thank you for confirming. Since no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child, let's continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Next question about where the children are living:**

Are either Alex or Blake currently in any of these special situations?

1. **In hospital** - staying in hospital for an extended period
2. **In care** - placed in local authority care
3. **Fostered** - in a foster care arrangement
4. **Normal living** - living at home or in a standard living situation

What is the current living situation for each child?
- **Alex (4)** - where does Alex live?
- **Blake (14)** - you mentioned Blake lives with you at home

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Blake lives with me at home - just normal living. Alex doesn't live with me but isn't in hospital, care, or fostered either. He just lives elsewhere, you know, in a normal situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: That's clear, thank you. Both children are in normal living situations - Blake with you, and Alex elsewhere but not in hospital, care, or fostering. Let me continue with the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
**Final question:**

Is the local council **NOT** paying towards the accommodation or maintenance for either Alex or Blake?

In other words, you're not receiving council payments specifically for housing or looking after either of the children?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything for either of them.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! That's all the checks completed. Let me now provide you with the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
---

## 🎉 Good News! You Are Eligible for Child Benefit

Based on the information you've provided, **you are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both Alex and Blake**.

### Summary for Each Child:

| Child | Age | Eligible? | Reason |
|-------|-----|-----------|--------|
| **Alex** | 4 | ✅ Yes | Under 16, you contribute to their upkeep, no other claimant |
| **Blake** | 14 | ✅ Yes | Under 16, lives with you, no other claimant |

---

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and each child
3. **Provide birth certificates** (or adoption certificates) for Alex and Blake

### Important Information:

- **Payments** are usually made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)
- ⚠️ **High Income Child Benefit Charge**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000 per year, you may need to pay back some or all of the benefit through a tax charge. You can still claim, but it's worth checking if this applies to you.

👉 **Apply here**: [www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim](https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim)

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex (age 4) meets all eligibility criteria: Under 16 years old (automatic age qualification), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and the local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance. You qualify as responsible for Alex because you contribute to their upkeep (clothes, etc.), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

Blake (age 14) meets all eligibility criteria: Under 16 years old (automatic age qualification), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with you in a normal living situation, and the local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance. You qualify as responsible for Blake because they live with you, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them.
arguments.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both of your children. You meet the claimant requirements as you normally live in the UK (England). Both children are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in apprenticeships, and the council isn't paying for either of them. You are responsible for Blake (lives with you) and Alex (you contribute to their upkeep), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex (age 4) meets all eligibility criteria: Under 16 years old (automatic age qualification), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, and the local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance. You qualify as responsible for Alex because you contribute to their upkeep (clothes, etc.), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

Blake (age 14) meets all eligibility criteria: Under 16 years old (automatic age qualification), not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in an apprenticeship, lives with you in a normal living situation, and the local council is not paying for their accommodation or maintenance. You qualify as responsible for Blake because they live with you, and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for them.

response.overall_reasoning: You are eligible to claim Child Benefit for both of your children. You meet the claimant requirements as you normally live in the UK (England). Both children are under 16, not working, not receiving benefits in their own right, not in apprenticeships, and the council isn't paying for either of them. You are responsible for Blake (lives with you) and Alex (you contribute to their upkeep), and no one else is claiming Child Benefit for either child.
